category: Occupational fraud - payroll scheme (ghost employees / diverted pay)
risk_signals: 'Employees' with no attendance, tax, or independent HR footprint (ghost workers)
Multiple salaries routed to the same or an employee-controlled bank account
Unexplained changes to payroll bank details shortly before pay runs
analyst_reasoning: Payroll schemes divert company funds through fake employees, inflated hours, or redirected salary payments. The signature is a payee that lacks the corroborating footprint of a real worker, or legitimate salaries quietly rerouted to an insider-controlled account.
investigation_steps: Reconcile the payroll register against HR, attendance, and tax records to find ghosts
Detect duplicate or employee-linked bank accounts receiving multiple salaries
Review payroll bank-detail change logs around pay-run dates
decision: Freeze the suspect payroll entries, audit the payroll-master change controls, and act on confirmed ghost or diversion entries.
outcome: Payroll fraud recurs every cycle until master-data controls detect it; HR-payroll reconciliation is the key control.
lessons_learned: A payee with no HR/tax footprint is the defining ghost-employee signal
Bank-detail changes just before pay runs are a payroll-diversion red flag
Payroll-master change control and periodic reconciliation are essential
